blurb: Colorado's showiest alpine primrose: vivid magenta flowers with a golden eye over big fleshy paddle leaves, lining icy snowmelt streams at 10,000–12,000 ft. Gorgeous but famously skunky-scented — and a hard high-alpine specialist to grow down in the garden.
water: High — constant cold moisture; grows in and beside snowmelt streams
seasons: Big fleshy paddle-shaped leaves; clusters of magenta golden-eyed flowers in mid-summer; dry seed capsules by late summer
wildlife: Nectar for alpine bumblebees (several Bombus spp.) and other high-country pollinators
deer: Rankly skunk-scented foliage is little browsed
toxic: Foliage can cause allergic contact dermatitis (the genus allergen primin) in sensitive people — handle with care; see Edible parts

care.hardiness: A true high-alpine perennial of wet subalpine-to-alpine ground at roughly 8,800–13,800 ft, ranging from Montana south through Colorado (where Charles Parry first collected it, in the alpine east of Middle Park) to Arizona and New Mexico.[1][2] It is cold-hardy far beyond anything a Boulder winter can throw at it, but its real limit down on the plains is heat, not cold — and it is famously difficult in cultivation: even a skilled high-altitude Colorado Springs grower found it 'makes very poor growth,' and one authority concluded it 'will never become a familiar plant in gardens.'[4]
care.planting: Grow it as the alpine bog specialist it is — a cool, constantly moist but sharply drained gritty pocket that stays cold at the root (a streamside, bog edge, or scree/rock-garden crevice), never a hot, dry border.[2][4] In a lowland Boulder garden give it cool afternoon shade and cold roots (a north aspect or the shaded, damp side of a water feature), keep it constantly moist, and expect it to be short-lived; it is far happier at elevation than on the plains.[4] Set out nursery-grown plants in spring after the mid-May frost and never let them dry out.[2]
care.sun: In its native alpine home it grows in full, intense high-altitude sun, but at low elevation that same sun cooks it; here give it filtered light or cool morning sun with afternoon shade, always with the roots kept cold and wet.[1][4]
care.soil: Indifferent to soil chemistry in the wild — it grows on granite, red shale, volcanic ash and even limestone cliffs — but always in cold seepage or permanently saturated, gritty ground; give it a moist, humus-rich but sharply drained gritty mix and avoid heavy, dry clay.[4][2]
care.water: A streamside/wetland obligate: in nature its roots sit in cold snowmelt seeps, waterfall ledges and stream margins, so it demands constant cold moisture and will not tolerate the dry spells an ordinary Front-Range perennial shrugs off.[2][5] Keep the soil permanently damp with cold water — supplying that steady coolness through a hot lowland summer is the whole challenge of growing it here.[2][4]
care.propagation: Propagate mainly from fresh seed; established crowns can be divided in spring but resent the disturbance, so seed is by far the usual route.[4]
care.sow: Like most alpine primulas, surface-sow the fine seed on a moist, gritty mix and keep it cool and constantly damp — sow in fall so winter supplies the chilling, or sow after cold treatment in spring.[4]
care.stratify: The seed germinates best after a spell of cold-moist chilling — leave a fall sowing out through winter, or give roughly 4–6 weeks in the refrigerator on damp grit before spring sowing.[4]
care.depth: Barely cover the tiny seed — press it onto the surface and keep it moist rather than burying it, as it needs light to germinate.[4]
care.bloom: Vivid magenta-crimson flowers, each with a yellow eye, in clusters of 5–25 on a stout leafless stalk in mid-summer (roughly July–August at elevation; later where snowbanks linger, earlier and lower down).[1][2][6]
care.maintenance: Very low where it is happy — the real work is simply keeping it cool and wet. Remove spent flower stalks if you don't want it to self-seed, and protect the crown from warm, stagnant winter wet in a lowland garden. Handle it with care: the whole plant is rankly skunk-scented, an odor released by even a light touch that can persist for years on dried material.[2][4]
care.selfsow: Where content it fills dry capsules with abundant seed and can self-sow into cool, wet pockets, but it is nowhere near weedy — getting it to establish at all off the mountains is the far harder problem.[2]
care.troubles: The overwhelming difficulty is climate, not pests: it is an alpine bog plant that hates lowland summer heat and drought, and even skilled high-altitude Colorado growers report it 'makes very poor growth' away from its native seeps.[4] Crown rot from warm, stagnant water and desiccation from any dry spell are the usual ways it is lost; give it cold running-water conditions, sharp drainage, and afternoon shade to have any chance here.[2][4]
care.companions: In the wild it shares cold snowmelt wetlands with marsh marigold and other alpine bog plants; in the garden pair it with other moisture-loving alpines in a cool bog, scree, or streamside planting.[2]

edible.summary: Not a food plant — Parry's primrose has no documented edible use, is famously skunk-scented, and, like others in its genus, can cause allergic skin reactions on handling.[2][7]
edible.caution: This alpine wildflower is not eaten and has no established edible use, so don't forage it. The whole plant is rankly skunk-scented — even a light touch releases a fetid, skunk-like odor that can persist for years on dried material.[2][4] Handle it with care as well: plants of the genus Primula are a well-known cause of allergic contact dermatitis, driven by the allergen primin concentrated in the glandular hairs on the leaves and stems, so wear gloves if you are sensitive.[7] On principle, high-alpine natives like this should never be dug, picked, or foraged from the wild — they are slow, fragile, and often protected on public land; enjoy them in place and grow only nursery-propagated seed.[1][2]
